title: Methodology
text:
We drew upon data from the Dutch Chamber of Commerce to build the Third Place Index. We classified all registered entities in the Dutch Chamber of Commerce as belonging to one of the following sectors:

Food & Beverage: e.g, restaurants, cafés, or bars
Retail: e.g., supermarkets, department stores, or small business
Community Centres: e.g., libraries, youth centres
Cultural Venues: e.g, cinemas, bookstores, or theatres
Sport & Leisure: e.g., gyms, or attraction parks
Medical Services: e.g., pharmacies, hospitals, or doctors

The exact codebook can be downloaded on Github: Download Sector Classification Codebook.



We calculated the number of Third Places of each of these sectors per PC-4 postcode. Then, we ranked the PC-4 postcode regions based on their number of Third Places in a given sector and calculated percentiles. This resulted in a score from 0 to 100. A value of 85 in the medical sector, for instance, means that this postcode has more medical services than 85% of the other postcodes. The data only contain this index and not the sum of Third Places per postcode as we could not export these numbers for postcodes with fewer than 10 companies due to the policy regulations of the CBS and of ODISSEI.
We also aggregated the sectors and calculated: - The total availability of Third Places - The total availability of Third Places excluding medical services - The total availability of Third Places excluding medical services and retail



Additionally, as the index — almost by definition — maps urban vs non-urban areas, we calculated each of these indices within the different levels of urbanisation. The

title: About
text:
Third Places
The term third place was coined by sociologist Ray Oldenburg in his 1989 book The Great Good Places. Third Places are spaces open to the public in which they can informally gather, mingle, and pass time, such as restaurants, cafés, bookstores, or libraries. The term “Third Place” stems from the distinction from “First Places” (the home) and “Second Places” (work and education). See Methodology for a full list of Third Places included in our definition.
The Third Place Index, following a publication from the US by Evan O’Neil, maps the availability of Third Places in the Netherlands. It gives a score to each area ranging from 0 to 100 describing how good the availability of Third Places is compared to other areas in the Netherlands. Low numbers indicate places with few Third Places and with higher danger of isolation while higher numbers indicate a wider availability of community building.
View the full description of the methodology

This project developed as part of the Summer Institute of Computational Social Science (SICSS) 2026 at ODISSEI Nederland. We want extend our gratitude to the organisers of the SICSS, most notably Tom Emery, Angelica Maineri, Javier Garcia Bernardo, and Tadas Bernotas.
